claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information about your situation.

First, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, how many children do you have?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have a child I'm responsible for. I have one child - my son Alex, who's 17 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'd like to understand Alex's current situation to assess your eligibility.

**Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?**

By full-time non-advanced education, I mean things like:
- A-levels or Scottish Highers
- T-levels
- NVQs up to level 3
- Home education (if started before he turned 16)

Or approved training programs.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex actually left school recently. He's just finished his education and he's signed up with the careers service to figure out what he wants to do next. He's in that sort of transition period where he's getting some guidance about his options.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. That's helpful information.

Just to be clear - **has Alex left education completely, or is he enrolled in approved training?** 

Also, **when exactly did he turn 17, and when did he leave education?** The specific dates are important for determining eligibility, as there are time limits that apply in these transition situations.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He turned 17 earlier this year and left education not long after that. He registered with the careers service pretty soon after leaving - it's been less than 20 weeks since he left school. He's not enrolled in any training yet, just getting advice from the careers service about what direction to take.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for those details. That's very helpful.

Let me ask a few more questions to complete the assessment:

1. **Are you (or your partner if you have one) currently responsible for Alex?** By this I mean - does he live with you, and do you provide for him?

2. **Are you or your partner resident in the UK?**

3. **Is there anyone else who might also be claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home and I'm responsible for him. I live in the UK - I'm in Leeds. And no, there's nobody else claiming Child Benefit for him, just me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me gather just one or two final details to complete the assessment:

1. **Do you have a partner, and if so, are they also resident in the UK?**

2. **Just to confirm - Alex is under 20 years old, correct?** (Since he's 17, I believe so, but I want to make sure)

3. **Is Alex in hospital or in local authority care, or living with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I don't have a partner - it's just me and Alex. Yes, he's definitely under 20, he's 17. And no, he's not in hospital or in care - he lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now make my assessment based on the UK Child Benefit rules.

**Assessment for Alex:**

Based on what you've told me, the key factors are:

- Alex is 17 years old (under 20) ✓
- He has left full-time education
- He registered with the careers service less than 20 weeks ago
- He is not currently in approved training or education
- You are responsible for him and he lives with you in the UK ✓
- No one else is claiming for him ✓

The rules state that for young people aged 16-19, there is an **extension period of up to 20 weeks** after they leave education or training, **but only if they are registered with the careers service or Connexions service AND they are under 18 years old**.

Since Alex turned 17 earlier this year and left education shortly after that, and he registered with the careers service less than 20 weeks ago, **you should still be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex during this extension period** - but only until either:
- The 20-week extension period ends, OR
- He turns 18, OR  
- He starts work of 24 hours or more per week

Once any of these happen, Child Benefit will stop unless he enrolls in approved education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Yes, you are currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

However, please be aware that this eligibility is temporary and will end when one of the following happens (whichever comes first):
- The 20-week extension period from when he registered with the careers service expires
- Alex turns 18
- Alex starts working 24 hours or more per week

**What happens next:**
If Alex enrolls in approved full-time non-advanced education or training before the extension period ends and before he turns 20, you can continue to claim Child Benefit for him until he turns 20 (as long as the education/training continues).

Is there anything else about Child Benefit that you'd like me to clarify?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old (under 20). He has left full-time education but registered with the careers service less than 20 weeks ago. Under UK Child Benefit rules, there is an extension period of up to 20 weeks for young people who have left education and registered with the careers service, provided they are under 18 years old. Since Alex is 17 and within the 20-week extension period, the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. However, this eligibility will end when: (1) the 20-week extension period expires, (2) Alex turns 18, or (3) Alex starts working 24+ hours per week - whichever comes first. The claimant is responsible for Alex, lives in the UK, and no one else is claiming for him, satisfying the other eligibility requirements.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is currently eligible for Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex, who recently left education and registered with the careers service less than 20 weeks ago. The 20-week extension period for young people registered with careers services applies, but this will end when the extension period expires, when Alex turns 18, or if he starts working 24+ hours per week.

Since Alex is 17 and within the 20-week extension period, the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. However, this eligibility will end when: (1) the 20-week extension period expires, (2) Alex turns 18, or (3) Alex starts working 24+ hours per week - whichever comes first. The claimant is responsible for Alex, lives in the UK, and no one else is claiming for him, satisfying the other eligibility requirements.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is currently eligible for Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex, who recently left education and registered with the careers service less than 20 weeks ago. The 20-week extension period for young people registered with careers services applies, but this will end when the extension period expires, when Alex turns 18, or if he starts working 24+ hours per week.
